feature.name: Programmatic scroll promises
feature.summary: Makes Element/Window scroll methods (scroll, scrollTo, scrollBy, scrollIntoView) return Promises that resolve on scroll completion, with the resolved value indicating whether the scroll was interrupted.

intake.finding: Problem is real, long-standing and recurring: developers cannot reliably know when a programmatic smooth-scroll has completed. The request dates to 2017 in CSSWG issue #1562, was formally resolved by the CSS WG in 2018 ('RESOLVED: add returning a promise to all scroll functions'), and has a Chromium tracking bug since 2018-08-08 (migrated from crbug 872341). The ChromeStatus entry, explainer and motivation articulate the same problem with concrete use cases (disable UI during scroll, highlight/focus after scroll, E2E test sequencing). Feature type is recorded as 'Chromium catches up' even though no engine had previously shipped it; this is a classification oddity rather than an intake defect.
intake.limitations: ChromeStatus record carries no feature_notes, measurement, adoption_expectation or availability_expectation; the intake documents the problem well but records no user-research or survey basis.

incubation.finding: Standards-level incubation is solid: the design was debated and resolved in CSSWG (#1562, then the follow-up #12495), with developer support in the GitHub thread (multiple +1s and use cases: analytics after scroll, focus-after-scroll, parallax). The explainer states goals, non-goals and contrasts the scrollend-event alternative. However ecosystem/customer research is thin: no developer survey, no interview cohort, no adjacent-platform/native analysis, and the explainer's Stakeholder Feedback/Opposition section is 'TBD'. Demand is inferred from a standards thread plus later case-study evidence, not from a validated research instrument.
incubation.limitations: No documented user research, survey or representative-cohort evidence; 'why browser intervention' is asserted via the CSSWG resolution rather than independently validated.

prototype.finding: A working implementation existed behind the runtime flag ProgrammaticScrollPromise (Element.idl June 2025, Window.idl July 2025) with a CodePen demo and WPT tests added February 2026. The implementation is substantial and includes deliberate code-health refactors and a dedicated scrollIntoView resolver. But there is no documented external developer trial, no friction log, no framework/server integration spike and no recruited cohort; prototype evidence is almost entirely internal CL history.
prototype.limitations: No public developer-trial findings, integration evidence or representative-user testing; 'works behind a flag' is not the same as 'validated by representative developers'.

developerTrials.finding: The feature did not run a public developer trial or recruit a cohort. Rationale: it is a backward-compatible additive change (return type undefined->Promise with a never-reject design to preserve 'ignore the return value' callers), judged low-risk enough to ship via behind-flag implementation plus WPT rather than a public dev trial. 'Not-relevant' here means the phase was deliberately bypassed for a compat-safe addition; it is NOT an assertion that the design was untested (WPT and behind-flag builds were used).
developerTrials.limitations: Skipping a trial means early real-world feedback (which later surfaced a scrollIntoView correctness issue in stable) was not collected before launch.

wideReview.finding: Mixed but net-positive review state. WebKit formally supports (position: support label by 2026-04-09). A Mozilla engineer (emilio) reviewed and the spec PR #12355 was merged with Mozilla approval, but Mozilla's formal standards position is still open with no label ('No signal'). Web-developer signal is recorded as Positive via #1562. WPT coverage exists. Gaps: the TAG review (#1210, filed March 2026) is still 'Pending' with no TAG position, and the follow-up normative issue #12495 (how to mark completion vs interruption, and scrollIntoView multi-scroller interruption semantics) was still open and labeled 'Needs Edits'/'Needs Testcase' at ship time.
wideReview.limitations: 'Support' position is not implementation; Mozilla 'No signal' is not neutral-positive; TAG review had not concluded; the normative detail other engines must implement was not yet final.

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial was run. Rationale: the change is additive and backward-compatible (no behavior removal, no new permissions, no resource cost), so no field uncertainty was judged to require real-origin/user conditions. The Intent to Prototype lists no OT plan. 'Not-relevant' means an OT was not warranted for this compat-safe addition, not that real-origin behavior was validated.
experiment.limitations: Had an OT been used, the scrollIntoView multi-scroller resolution path could have been exercised on real sites before stable.

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship posted 2026-03-31 received three LGTMs within a day (Alex Russell, Daniel Bratell, Dan Clark) by 2026-04-01. Spec PR merged, WPT coverage in place, Element/Window/scrollIntoView all implemented, Finch name set (ProgrammaticScrollPromise), rollout plan 'enabled for all users', breaking_change=false, enterprise_impact none. The enable CL landed on main and the team resolved a use-after-free (deferred promise resolution) before stable, including an M149-branch backport. Compat analysis is present and explicit (avoid rejection; one documented async-throw edge case).
prepareToShip.limitations: Enablement assets were minimal at gate time (release-notes paragraph + CodePen); a milestone ambiguity (M149 backport vs ChromeStatus M150) was not reconciled in public materials.

release.finding: Shipped on schedule: Chrome 150 beta 2026-06-02, stable 2026-06-30, listed in the Chrome 150 release notes (DOM and HTML) with ChromeStatus and spec links. MDN per-method pages document the Promise return value and the 'interrupted' property, MDN browser-compat-data carries a Chrome 150 entry, and a caniuse page exists for the Window scroll promise subfeature. Default-enabled on desktop, Android and WebView (iOS null).
release.limitations: Communication was release-notes-level: no dedicated developer.chrome.com/web.dev deep-dive or official demo beyond the CodePen; caniuse extraction of exact version numbers was inconsistent across retrievals (Chrome 150 corroborated by four independent sources).

adoption.finding: Feature is only ~3 weeks past stable at the evidence cutoff, so durable adoption cannot be assessed. No public use-counter/usage data is available yet, and no framework, library or build-tool integration was found. The only substantive independent developer evidence is a July 2026 ICS MEDIA article that implements real use cases (table-of-contents highlight after scroll, guided tour, interruption handling) and a community MDN doc-report flagging the missing return-value documentation. No attributable production deployment evidence exists. Adoption must remain unknown; it must not be inferred from shipment or from one article.
adoption.limitations: No usage metrics, no framework integration, no partner/production case study with denominator; too early to score.

support.finding: The tracking bug is active and comprehensive and the team fixed a late UAF and built a dedicated scrollIntoView resolver (bug 399877341). However early external evidence shows quality friction: an independent developer reported in Chrome 150.0.7871.46 stable that scrollIntoView() resolves its Promise before scrolling has completed (the hardest multi-scroller case and the subject of the still-open spec issue #12495). Known edge cases include the async-throw regression for malformed scroll calls. No public support-issue dashboard or recurrence metrics are visible.
support.limitations: Support burden and recurrence are not yet measurable; one third-party report does not establish prevalence, but it confirms a real open correctness issue in the shipped version.

deprecation.finding: This is an additive new capability, not a deprecation or removal: nothing is removed, no migration is required, and breaking_change is false with no enterprise impact. 'Not-relevant' means the phase does not apply to this launch; it is not used to evade scrutiny of the additive change.
deprecation.limitations: None material; the backward-compatible non-rejection design is what makes a deprecation phase inapplicable.

developerValue.finding: Addresses a real, recurring, multi-year developer problem (CSSWG-resolved in 2018) with a clean, ergonomic solution: `await element.scrollTo(...)` replaces fragile scrollend/setTimeout/IntersectionObserver workarounds that the ICS MEDIA case study confirms are error-prone (scrollend does not fire for no-op scrolls and fires per-scroller for scrollIntoView; setTimeout mistimes by distance). Use cases (disable UI during scroll, highlight/focus after scroll, router scroll-before-commit, E2E scroll-then-assert) are concrete and widely applicable.
developerValue.limitations: No formal developer survey or user research underpins the demand; evidence is a standards thread plus one independent case study.

endUserImpact.finding: Benefit is indirect and developer-mediated: end users get more reliable, better-timed scroll-driven UX (highlights/focus land correctly, no setTimeout-induced jank) at no new permission, privacy or resource cost. The feature adds no end-user control. A gap is that the explainer carries no accessibility analysis even though smooth scrolling interacts with prefers-reduced-motion (under 'reduce', smooth scrolls become instant and the Promise resolves immediately); this interaction is unaudited and could matter for vestibular-sensitive users if developers rely on promise timing.
endUserImpact.limitations: No measured end-user impact; accessibility/reduced-motion angle was neither analyzed in the explainer nor tested publicly.

adoption.finding: Too early to score: ~3 weeks past stable, no public usage data, no framework/library integration, no attributable production deployment. One independent developer article demonstrates experimentation. Per method, adoption must remain unscored rather than inferred from shipment, press sentiment, or the absence of criticism.
adoption.limitations: No usage metrics or production case studies with denominator exist at the cutoff.

interoperability.finding: Standards-aligned (CSSWG resolution, merged spec PR in cssom-view, Working Draft maturity) with positive vendor signals (WebKit support; Mozilla engineer approved the spec PR). WPT tests exist and are synced into Firefox's tree. BUT it shipped Chrome/Edge-only (150+) and is NOT Baseline: Firefox and Safari have not implemented it. The Firefox activity is test-only WPT sync (Bugzilla wpt-sync), not a feature implementation. The follow-up normative issue #12495 (completion-vs-interruption and scrollIntoView semantics) was still open ('Needs Edits') at ship, raising the risk that other engines implement divergent semantics. The 'Chromium catches up' feature-type label is misleading: Chrome is first, not catching up to another engine.
interoperability.limitations: 'Support' position is not implementation; cross-engine behavioral interop is unproven because no second engine ships the feature.

implementationQuality.finding: Careful, compat-conscious core: non-rejection design preserves 'ignore the return value' callers, multiple code-health refactors, a dedicated scrollIntoView multi-scroller resolver, WPT coverage and three LGTMs. Against that: a use-after-free in promise resolution was caught and fixed only just before stable (and backported to the M149 branch), a known compat regression exists (malformed scroll calls now throw asynchronously rather than synchronously), and an independent developer reproduced a scrollIntoView premature-resolution bug in Chrome 150 stable. These are real late-stage defects in a shipped API.
implementationQuality.limitations: Prevalence of the scrollIntoView issue is unknown; memory-safety and correctness defects were addressed but indicate the scrollIntoView/multi-scroller path was under-exercised pre-ship.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Core narrative is coherent and consistent across ChromeStatus, explainer, Intent threads and spec. MDN/BCD/caniuse documentation landed at launch. However: the explainer is stale relative to the shipped API (it shows Promise<undefined>; the shipped value is an object with an 'interrupted' boolean) and has 'Stakeholder Feedback/Opposition: TBD' plus no security/privacy/accessibility sections; enablement is a release-notes paragraph plus a CodePen with no dedicated DevRel article; milestone ambiguity (M149 backport and third-party M149 citations vs ChromeStatus M150) was not reconciled publicly; and Mozilla's 'No signal' plus the open spec issue understate residual uncertainty in launch materials.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: No evidence of factually incorrect Chrome-authored claims, but the explainer drift and thin enablement lower communication quality for a newly-available, single-engine API.

support.finding: Active maintenance via the tracking bug: the UAF was fixed (with branch backport) and the scrollIntoView path got a dedicated fix and tests. Early external friction is real but bounded (one independent report of scrollIntoView premature resolution in stable; one documented async-throw edge case). No public support dashboard or recurrence metrics yet, so burden cannot be quantified.
support.limitations: Support health cannot be scored beyond 'issues exist and are being worked'; prevalence and resolution-time data are not public.

overall.finding: A well-motivated, standards-aligned, backward-compatible addition that shipped on schedule with MDN/caniuse docs and positive vendor signals. It is held to 'mixed' (not success) because: it is single-engine at launch (not Baseline, despite a misleading 'catches up' label); the spec's fulfillment-semantics detail was still open at ship; a late UAF and a residual scrollIntoView correctness bug reached stable; enablement/explainer quality is thin and partly stale; and adoption/usage cannot yet be assessed. Shipment, positive press and absence of loud criticism are not treated as success.
overall.limitations: Overall reflects evidence at ~3 weeks post-stable; durable adoption and cross-engine interop remain unknown and would warrant a dated revision.

supports: Feature metadata, owners, Blink>Scroll component, finch name ProgrammaticScrollPromise, desktop/android/webview=150, iOS=null, breaking_change=false, enterprise_impact none, feature_type 'Chromium catches up', Mozilla No signal, WebKit Support, Web developers Positive, spec under development in WG, no origin trial/dev trial
sources.limitations: accurate_as_of 2026-06-08; is_released=false and status 'Proposed' in the snapshot despite M150 ship, a known ChromeStatus staleness artifact.

supports: Goals/non-goals/use cases, Promise-based design, non-rejection rationale, scrollend alternative rejected, CodePen sample
sources.limitations: Explainer is stale vs shipped API: shows Promise<undefined> rather than the shipped {interrupted:boolean}; 'Stakeholder Feedback/Opposition: TBD'; no security/privacy/accessibility sections.

supports: Original 2017 request, multi-year developer demand (analytics, focus-after-scroll, parallax), CSSWG resolution 'RESOLVED: add returning a promise to all scroll functions', Web-developer positive signal
sources.limitations: Thread-level demand, not a validated survey; exact resolution date inferred from referenced comment id.

supports: Spec change switching return types to Promise, merged; Mozilla (emilio) involvement/approval; evolved to dictionary value
sources.limitations: PR evolved during review (undefined -> boolean -> dictionary); merged date approximate from commit references (Oct-Nov 2025).

supports: Follow-up normative open questions: completion-vs-interruption marking, same-scroller interruption scope, scrollIntoView multi-scroller semantics; labels 'Needs Edits'/'Needs Testcase (WPT)' as of 2026-03-16, i.e. still open at ship
sources.limitations: Status is point-in-time; later resolution would warrant a dated revision.

supports: TAG design-review request filed by CSSWG; references explainer, WPT, vendor issues; TAG review status Pending in ChromeStatus
sources.limitations: No TAG position outcome found at cutoff; review requested by the WG rather than the feature team.

supports: Mozilla standards-position issue opened 2026-03-24, state open, no formal position label ('No signal'); references emilio as Mozillian input
sources.limitations: No formal Mozilla position label; spec-PR approval by a Mozilla engineer is a separate, weaker signal than a position.

supports: WebKit position label 'support' (created 2026-03-24, updated 2026-04-09); positive follow-up in CSSWG
sources.limitations: 'support' = conceptually good and worth prototyping; it is not a shipping commitment or implementation.

supports: Intent to Prototype 2025-06-10; motivation; Interop risk rationale; Gecko/WebKit 'No signal' at that time; Web developers Positive; no OT planned; tracking bug 41406914
sources.limitations: Google Groups extraction can truncate; vendor signals are as of June 2025 (WebKit later moved to support).

supports: Intent to Ship 2026-03-31; three LGTMs by 2026-04-01 (Alex Russell, Daniel Bratell, Dan Clark); WebKit Support, Mozilla No signal noted at ship
sources.limitations: Google Groups extraction can truncate; thread also surfaced a mailing-list delivery complaint unrelated to the feature.

supports: Bug created 2018-08-08 (migrated from 872341), Status Fixed; full CL timeline: behind-flag Element.idl (2025-06-24) and Window.idl (2025-07-16), WPTs (2026-02-09), scrollBy/Window hookup (Feb-Mar 2026), dictionary resolution (2026-03-02), scrollIntoView resolver (2026-04-09), interruption exposure (2026-04-27), UAF fix (2026-05-06) + M149 backport (2026-05-07); async-throw regression noted in CL #17
sources.limitations: Issue tracker extract is point-in-time and omits some private discussion; exact merge/branch mapping inferred.

supports: Chrome 150 stable 2026-06-30; feature listed under DOM and HTML with ChromeStatus + spec links; default-enabled Android/ChromeOS/Linux/macOS/Windows
sources.limitations: One-paragraph release-notes treatment; no dedicated deep-dive.

supports: Chrome 150 beta 2026-06-02; beta-cycle timing
sources.limitations: Beta blog; not feature-specific deep-dive.

supports: MDN documents Promise return value resolving to an object with 'interrupted' boolean for Element.scroll/scrollTo/scrollBy and Window equivalents; usage guidance tied to behavior:smooth
sources.limitations: MDN pages are community-maintained and were updated around the Chrome 150 ship; no standalone landing page for the feature.

supports: Community report that return-value section omitted the Promise and requested a BCD subfeature; reporter cited 'Chrome/Edge 149' (milestone error)
sources.limitations: Reporter's milestone claim (149) conflicts with authoritative M150 sources; issue date not captured.

supports: BCD commit 'Chrome 150 scroll methods return promises' adding the Promise subfeature
sources.limitations: Commit timestamp not captured; reflects Chrome/Edge 150 only.

supports: Dedicated caniuse page tracks the Promise-return subfeature; Chrome/Edge 150+ supported; Safari/Firefox not supported
sources.limitations: Exact version numbers were extracted inconsistently across retrievals; Chrome 150 corroborated by S1/S12/S14/S16.

supports: Independent developer case study (guide tour, highlight-after-scroll, interruption handling via result.interrupted) replacing scrollend/setTimeout/IntersectionObserver; validates developer value; reports scrollIntoView() resolves its Promise before scrolling completes in Chrome 150.0.7871.46 / macOS 26.5.1 / MacBook Air M1 (2020); Chrome/Edge 150+ only as of July 2026
sources.limitations: Single author/environment; prevalence of the scrollIntoView bug not established; one source.

supports: Press coverage promoting the feature and use cases (router scroll, E2E tests, infinite scroll); cites Chrome 149 (erroneous milestone) and a resolved-value description that differs slightly from shipped {interrupted:boolean}
sources.limitations: Milestone error (149 vs authoritative 150); partially repeats announcement framing; secondary, not independent primary evidence.

supports: 'Enable programmatic scroll promises' CL linking ChromeStatus and Intent to Ship; Fixed: 41406914
sources.limitations: Enable-on-main CL; branch/milestone mapping inferred (M150 stable per S1/S12).

supports: scrollIntoView promise-resolution CL (ActiveScrollTracker across multiple ScrollableAreas); Fixed bug 399877341; adds scrollIntoView WPT coverage incl. interruptions
sources.limitations: Confirms scrollIntoView was the hard multi-scroller case requiring a dedicated fix; residual issue still reported in stable (S18).

supports: UAF fix deferring promise resolution to a post-task (ProgrammaticScrollAnimator GC); M149-branch backport (refs/branch-heads/7827) on 2026-05-07
sources.limitations: Existence of an M149 backport of this code contributed to public milestone ambiguity (S19/S15).

supports: Promisify element.idl behind flag ProgrammaticScrollPromise; documented exposed regression: malformed element.scroll*() no longer throws synchronously, returns a Promise that rejects asynchronously
sources.limitations: Regression is an edge case; prevalence unknown.

supports: WPT tests added (element/window scroll-promise tests; later scrollIntoView interruption tests via S21)
sources.limitations: WPT results dashboard (wpt.fyi) not re-verified at cutoff.

supports: Mozilla wpt-sync of PR 58301 (window scroll promise tests) into mozilla-central, tracked fixed in firefox150 branch
sources.limitations: This is a TEST-ONLY sync, NOT a Firefox feature implementation; must not be read as Firefox shipping the feature.

supports: Official sample/demo referenced by explainer and TAG review; demonstrates promise-based scroll
sources.limitations: CodePen only; no richer official demo or dedicated DevRel article found.

supports: NOT USED for claims — recorded only to document an unreliable source
sources.limitations: UNRELIABLE: reported Chrome/Edge/Firefox 130 and Safari 18, contradicting S1/S12/S14/S16/S17/S18; the feature is unmapped in the web-features registry (ChromeStatus web_feature 'Missing feature'), which causes webstatus.dev to misreport. Excluded from all findings.

successes: Solved a real, multi-year, CSSWG-resolved developer problem (knowing when a smooth scroll completes) with a clean, ergonomic, awaitable API.
Backward-compatible by design: non-rejection of returned Promises preserves the large base of callers that ignore the return value.
Positive standards/vendor signals at ship: WebKit 'support', Mozilla spec-PR approval, merged cssom-view spec PR, WPT coverage.
Careful engineering on the hard parts: dedicated scrollIntoView multi-scroller resolver, and a late use-after-free caught and fixed (with branch backport) before broad exposure.
Documentation landed at launch: MDN per-method pages, BCD Chrome 150 entry, and a caniuse subfeature page; shipped on schedule (beta 2026-06-02, stable 2026-06-30).
failures: Shipped single-engine: Chrome/Edge only (150+); Firefox and Safari have not implemented, so the feature is NOT Baseline despite a misleading 'Chromium catches up' feature-type label.

Shipped with the spec's fulfillment-semantics detail still open: CSSWG #12495 (completion-vs-interruption marking and scrollIntoView multi-scroller interruption) was labeled 'Needs Edits'/'Needs Testcase' at ship, leaving normative ambiguity for other implementers.
A residual scrollIntoView correctness bug (Promise resolves before scroll completes) reached Chrome 150 stable and was independently reproduced by a developer.
A use-after-free in promise resolution and a synchronous-to-asynchronous throw compat regression indicate the scrollIntoView/multi-scroller path was under-exercised before ship.

Enablement and explainer quality are thin/partly stale: no dedicated DevRel article (release-notes paragraph + CodePen only), explainer still shows Promise<undefined> (shipped value is {interrupted:boolean}), and has no security/privacy/accessibility sections; public milestone ambiguity (M149 vs M150) was not reconciled.
counterfactuals: If a small, deliberately-varied developer trial (or a real-origin exercise of scrollIntoView across nested scrollers) had preceded ship, the scrollIntoView premature-resolution bug reported in stable by ICS MEDIA would likely have been caught earlier.

If the follow-up spec issue #12495 (completion-vs-interruption and scrollIntoView semantics) had been fully resolved before ship, other engines would face lower implementation-divergence risk and might ship sooner, moving the feature toward Baseline faster.

If the explainer had carried security/privacy/accessibility sections and addressed the prefers-reduced-motion interaction (smooth->instant makes the Promise resolve immediately), the launch would have pre-empted an unaudited accessibility angle.
If a dedicated DevRel article plus an official demo (beyond CodePen) had shipped alongside the release notes, early developer understanding and credible adoption evidence would be stronger.

If milestone traceability had been enforced (reconciling the M149-branch backport and third-party M149 citations against ChromeStatus M150), the public confusion about which Chrome version first enabled the feature would not have occurred.
skillImprovements: prepareToShip/release phase packets should require an explainer-vs-shipped-API reconciliation before launch; flag when an explainer shows a different resolved-value shape (here Promise<undefined> vs {interrupted:boolean}) or still says 'Stakeholder Feedback: TBD'.

wideReview module must distinguish a standards-position 'open/no-label' (Mozilla here) and a spec-PR approval from a formal position, and must never let WebKit 'support' + Mozilla spec-PR approval be reported as broad interop when no second engine has implemented.

Add an interop/Baseline rule: for features with no prior engine implementation, mark Baseline as Limited/Newly Available and do NOT inherit the base feature's Baseline status (the scroll-elements base feature is Widely Available; the Promise subfeature is not).
Add a regression-eval for additive return-type changes (undefined->Promise): surface the synchronous-to-asynchronous throw compat shift as a mandatory known-issue in launch comms and MDN.
Add a source-disambiguation eval: treat Mozilla/Firefox wpt-sync Bugzilla entries (test-only imports) as distinct from engine implementation, to avoid over-counting interoperability.

Release-comms asset threshold: any feature shipping to stable should have at least one dedicated DevRel article/official demo in addition to a release-notes paragraph; flag when only a CodePen + paragraph exist.

Add a milestone-traceability check to the release packet: when a stable-branch backport exists or third parties cite a milestone different from ChromeStatus, flag and reconcile before publishing release notes so I2S/ChromeStatus/release-notes agree.

Add an accessibility-lens prompt to the prototype/wide-review packets for any motion- or scroll-related API: explicitly analyze interaction with prefers-reduced-motion and document the result in the explainer.

missingEvidence: Public usage/use-counter adoption data (feature ~3 weeks past stable; not yet available)
Framework/library/build-tool integration evidence (none found; likely too early)
Attributable production deployment case studies beyond one independent developer article
Other-engine (Firefox/Safari) implementation timing and whether they adopt the still-open #12495 semantics
TAG review outcome (#1210 was Pending at cutoff; no TAG position recorded)
